title: exec: remove polling for PID file
body:
a couple of improvements for exec:

- we use fsnotify to take advantage of inotify on Linux so we don't poll for the file being created
- if the OCI runtime exited don't let the timeout expire first, but return an error immediately.

We recently bumped the timeout value, that means we could block the container lock for 60s if the runtime failed

@giuseppe, the linter isn't happy:
```
libpod/container_api.go:349:10:warning: if block ends with a return statement, so drop this else and outdent its block (golint)
```

Is fsnotify safe to use for this? There is a (if I recall correctly, fairly
low) global cap on inotify sessions, and since we're not a daemon we can
probably exhaust those if a lot of exec sessions are running

@mheon From googling around their looks like their is max watch of a directory.  The default is 8192.

```
cat /proc/sys/fs/inotify/max_user_watches
8192
```

I think we are safe with this change.

I think there is a per-user name as well which is substantially lower

More general comment - we should retain the timeout even if we use inotify
here. The exit file might not be created for various reasons on the conmon
side, and we don't want to hang forever

to avoid any kind of issue with hitting the inotify limit, I've changed the implementation to fallback to polling if the watcher cannot be created.

The timeout is still honored, this change only adds another check for the OCI process.  If the runtime has exited there is no need to let the timeout expire and we can assume the PID file is not going to be written as there is no process running.
@giuseppe Seems to be causing issues with checkpointing?
rebased.  I've lost track of all the PRs that are blocked because of PAPR failures since weeks.
Yup that is why I am going through the old ones, to see if we can get some stuff merged  but of course the CI is crashing again.  :^(
